title: No test covers a partially-recognized apply_patch envelope that silently drops an unrecognized directive instead of failing closed
evidence:
parse_apply_patch's line loop (core logic at plugins/ca-codex/hooks/_host.py:116-145) silently skips any line that doesn't match a recognized marker (`_flush` only ever runs at a recognized _ADD/_DELETE/_UPDATE boundary or at end-of-text) — an unrecognized '*** ' directive INSIDE an otherwise-valid envelope neither aborts parsing nor contributes an op; it is just dropped, and iter_file_ops' fail-closed 'opaque' fallback (_host.py:233-239) only fires when the returned ops list is EMPTY. Reproduced directly: `parse_apply_patch('*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: ok.txt
+hello
*** Rename File: secret/.codearbiter/overrides.log
*** End Patch
')` returns exactly one op (`ok.txt`, write) — the second (unrecognized) directive's file vanishes with no opaque/blocked signal, and the call site sees a non-empty, apparently-complete ops list. The whole-unit read of .github/scripts/test_codex_adapter.py's TestApplyPatchParsing class confirms every fixture is either fully-recognized (test_add_file, test_update_file_extracts_only_added_lines, test_combined_patch_yields_all_ops, etc.) or fully-UNrecognized (test_envelope_with_zero_ops_fails_closed_as_opaque uses an envelope with ZERO recognized directives) — no fixture mixes one recognized op with one unrecognized directive, so this partial-drop path is never exercised.
impact: A Codex apply_patch envelope containing one ordinary file op plus one directive shape the adapter doesn't recognize (a parser-grammar drift, a future Codex directive, or an adversarial envelope crafted to exploit the gap) returns a non-empty, seemingly-valid ops list — the fail-closed opaque guard never engages, and the unrecognized file's write/delete/rename reaches disk completely unguarded while the visible op looks legitimate. This is the exact guard-bypass class the module's own docstring (_host.py:78-82) warns against ('under-reporting a whole envelope would un-guard every file in it') but the mitigation is unverified by any test.
recommendation: Add a TestApplyPatchParsing fixture mixing one recognized op with one unrecognized '*** ' directive line and assert the CURRENT behavior explicitly (documents the drop, forces a deliberate decision), then decide whether parse_apply_patch should instead treat an unrecognized '*** '-prefixed line as an abort signal (fail closed to opaque) rather than a skip — a mid-envelope directive it cannot classify is a stronger closed-guard trigger than the current zero-ops-only check.
acceptance_criteria: A test exists asserting the exact ops returned for an envelope with one recognized Add/Update/Delete File plus one unrecognized '*** ' directive line
The test's assertion matches whatever the deliberate fix decision is (either explicit-drop-is-acceptable-and-documented, or fail-closed-to-opaque)
